import Callout from '~/components/Callout.astro';

If you've used a Cortex-M, statistically you've used an STM32. STMicroelectronics ships >2 billion STM32 units a year across a dozen families, from ultra-low-cost C0 to the 480 MHz dual-core H7. The ecosystem (HAL, CubeMX, CubeIDE, Discovery/Nucleo boards, documentation) is rough around the edges but **vast** — for almost any problem there's a HAL example, an AN-XXXX application note, or a community post.

## The families

| Family    | Core                | Clock           | Position              |
|-----------|---------------------|-----------------|-----------------------|
| **STM32C0** | Cortex-M0+        | 48 MHz          | Lowest cost, 8-bit replacement |
| **STM32F0** | Cortex-M0          | 48 MHz          | Mainstream entry, legacy        |
| **STM32G0** | Cortex-M0+         | 64 MHz          | Modern mainstream entry, C0's bigger sibling |
| **STM32L0** | Cortex-M0+         | 32 MHz          | Low-power M0+                 |
| **STM32F1** | Cortex-M3          | 72 MHz          | Legacy classic, still shipping |
| **STM32F2** | Cortex-M3          | 120 MHz         | Connectivity (Ethernet)        |
| **STM32F3** | Cortex-M4          | 72 MHz          | Mixed-signal + analog peripherals |
| **STM32F4** | Cortex-M4          | 84–180 MHz      | The workhorse — most popular M4 in the world |
| **STM32F7** | Cortex-M7          | 216 MHz         | High-end M7 + L1 cache         |
| **STM32G4** | Cortex-M4          | 170 MHz         | Motor control + digital power  |
| **STM32H7** | Cortex-M7 (+M4)    | 480 MHz         | Top-end perf, single or dual-core |
| **STM32H5** | Cortex-M33         | 250 MHz         | M33 successor to F4/F7         |
| **STM32L4** | Cortex-M4          | 80 MHz          | Mainstream low-power           |
| **STM32L5** | Cortex-M33         | 110 MHz         | Low-power + TrustZone          |
| **STM32U5** | Cortex-M33         | 160 MHz         | Modern mainstream, TrustZone-first |
| **STM32WB** | Cortex-M4 + M0+    | 64 MHz          | Wireless (BLE) + app core      |
| **STM32WL** | Cortex-M4 (+M0+)   | 48 MHz          | LoRa / sub-GHz                 |
| **STM32MP** | Cortex-A7 + M4     | 800 MHz / 209 MHz | Linux + RT companion         |

## How to pick a line

- **"I want cheapest possible 32-bit"** → STM32C0 ($0.30–$1.00).
- **"I want a general-purpose modern entry MCU"** → STM32G0.
- **"I want maximum ecosystem for an M4 mid-range design"** → STM32F4 (specifically F411 or F446).
- **"I want maximum performance"** → STM32H7 (single-core 480 MHz) or H7 dual-core (M7 + M4 helper).
- **"I want low-power for a battery-powered IoT device"** → STM32L4 (older) or STM32U5 (newer, recommended).
- **"I want TrustZone for secure boot / PSA"** → STM32U5 or L5.
- **"I want motor control or power"** → STM32G4.
- **"I want Bluetooth Low Energy on chip"** → STM32WB.
- **"I want LoRa on chip"** → STM32WL.
- **"I need Linux + real-time"** → STM32MP (Cortex-A7 + Cortex-M4 on one die).

## The ecosystem

### CubeMX

GUI configurator. You pick a chip, click through pin assignments and peripheral configurations, and it generates a project (Makefile, CMake, IDE-specific) with the HAL code initialised. Pretty good for bring-up; opinionated about HAL usage. Many production teams generate CubeMX code once for the init scaffolding and then write their own peripheral drivers on top.

### HAL vs LL

**HAL** — the "Hardware Abstraction Layer". Higher level, callback-based, portable across STM32 families. `HAL_UART_Init`, `HAL_UART_Transmit_DMA`. Reasonable performance, plenty of code overhead.

**LL** — "Low-Layer". Inline functions directly mapping to register writes. Same as writing the registers yourself but with named macros. Faster, smaller, less portable.

Use HAL for fast development. Drop to LL (or raw registers via CMSIS) for hot paths.

### CubeIDE

Eclipse-based IDE bundled by ST. Free. Works fine but slower than CLion/VS Code for the same projects. Most production teams use CubeIDE for the integrated CubeMX experience and then VS Code with CMake for daily work.

### Documentation

Every chip has:
- **Datasheet** — pinout, electrical characteristics, package info.
- **Reference Manual (RM)** — register-level details. Often 1500+ pages.
- **Programming Manual (PM)** — Cortex-M ISA reference (mostly redundant with ARM's docs).
- **Application Notes (AN)** — per-topic deep dives. The hidden gold; there are excellent ANs on motor control, USB, secure boot, RTC.
- **Errata sheet** — bugs in the silicon. Always check.

The RM is your daily reference once you're past bring-up. Bookmark it.

## STM32-specific things to know

### Two PLLs and a clock tree to study

The clock tree on STM32H7 has three PLLs feeding multiple bus and peripheral domains. Getting clocks right is the first hard problem on any STM32 project. CubeMX has a graphical clock-tree configurator — use it.

### `BSRR` is your friend

The Bit Set/Reset Register on every STM32 GPIO is **atomic** — setting and clearing pins doesn't need read-modify-write. Use it instead of `ODR |= ...`. See [GPIO](/docs/peripherals/gpio/).

### Boot pins

`BOOT0` (and on some chips `BOOT1`) select the boot mode: from flash, from system memory (ROM bootloader), or from SRAM. The ROM bootloader supports flashing over UART, USB DFU, SPI, I²C, CAN. Saves you from a brick if SWD ever fails.

### Option bytes and RDP

Each STM32 has **option bytes** controlling watchdog config, brown-out, write protection, and **Read Out Protection (RDP)**:
- **RDP Level 0** — fully open.
- **RDP Level 1** — flash read disabled via SWD, but you can revert to Level 0 (mass-erasing the chip).
- **RDP Level 2** — permanently locked. SWD disabled forever. **Cannot be undone.**

Set Level 2 only on production binaries — and only on chips you've already qualified. Test extensively in Level 1 first.

<Callout type="warn" title="RDP Level 2 is irreversible. Once set, you can't recover via SWD.">
Many an engineer has bricked dev boards by accidentally setting RDP 2 on the wrong chip. Use script gates and double-confirmation in your factory programming flow.
</Callout>
